1:
The chapter opens on the great bronze altar, and the witnesses read it at once as history and as gospel shadow. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fastens on a detail the older book of Kings had passed over — for the making of this altar, he observes, is not recorded there at all — and on its sheer scale. The altar Moses made in the tabernacle had been five cubits square; this was twenty cubits square and ten cubits high, a capacious scaffold enlarged, Henry reasons, because Israel had grown more numerous and more rich, and it was hoped more devout, so that a greater abundance of offerings might be brought and none might excuse himself by pleading there was no room. "God had greatly enlarged their borders; it was therefore fit that they should enlarge his altars," he writes — our returns should bear some proportion to our receivings. The ten cubits of height he takes for a mercy to the worshippers: raised so high, the people in the courts could see the sacrifice burn, and the eye might affect the heart with sorrow for sin — "It is of the Lord's mercies that I am not thus consumed."

Above all Henry reads the altar toward Christ. On it every sacrifice was offered, and it sanctified the gift; yet the blood of bulls and goats could not possibly take away sin, and so the burning victim was meant to lead the worshipper to consider the great Sacrifice to be offered in the fulness of time, the propitiation on which faith must keep its eye fixed in all our devotions. With the smoke of the offerings, he adds, the heart might ascend to heaven in holy desires toward God and his favour. Henry attends even to the approach: how they carried the sacrifices up to so high an altar the text does not say, but if by steps, these were surely so contrived that the end of the law given in Exodus 20:26 — that no nakedness be discovered upon God's altar — might be answered.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the chapter's frame, laying out in order what Huram and Solomon would make: the bronze altar here, then the molten sea and its supports, the ten lavers, the ten golden candlesticks, the ten tables and the hundred golden basins, the priests' court, and at last all the vessels of gold and bronze for the house of God. The two witnesses do not divide. Together they set the altar as the threshold of the whole account, the place where the enlarged worship of an enlarged people begins — and where, for Henry, the reader is taught to look past the bronze and the smoke to the one propitiation the whole apparatus was built to foreshadow.
2:
"Also he made a molten sea of ten cubits from brim to brim, round in compass" — and the description draws the grammatical-historical witnesses into their characteristic work of comparing the chronicler word for word with the older account in 1 Kings 7:23. Keil and Delitzsch identify it as the same great bronze basin described there, and treat the small divergences as the ordinary wear of transmission rather than real differences of fact: where the chronicler writes bekarim and the older text pekaim, or where the sea is said to hold three thousand baths instead of two thousand, they judge these the result of orthographical errors that crept into the Chronicle. One phrase in the fifth verse — "it received and held" — they defend against the charge of being a gloss borrowed from the parallel, allowing it to stand as a merely pleonastic "receiving baths, three thousand it held."

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, works at the same level of the letter. Taking up a nicety that will recur at the chapter's close, he notes that even where the older text may be correct, the chronicler could have understood a rare word to mean "openings" rather than "hinges," and so have substituted a commoner term of the same sense — the resemblance of the one word to the other being, by ancient notions of interpretation, a further reason in its favour. It is a small observation, but it exposes the method the whole chapter will reward: the Chronicler is not inventing but re-copying and lightly smoothing an older record, and the differences are to be weighed as a scribe's work, not a historian's contradiction.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts his eyes from the letter to the thing. The molten sea, he says, was a very large brass pan in which water was kept for the priests to wash in, and it was set just at the entrance into the court of the priests, like the font at the church door — the first thing met on the way in, a standing summons to purity at the very threshold of worship. The witnesses do not contend. The grammatical-historical pair guard the text and its numbers; Henry reads the vessel they are guarding as a sign that those who would draw near to serve must first be washed, a theme the next verses on its ornament and its capacity will only deepen.
3:
Beneath the rim of the sea ran a band of figured oxen, "ten in a cubit, compassing the sea round about," cast in two rows — and here the grammatical-historical witnesses fall into a genuine dispute over the text. The parallel in 1 Kings 7:24 reads not "oxen" but "wild gourds," and the two Hebrew words are so alike that a copyist might easily confuse them. Adam Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian voice, leans toward correction: the reading of the older text is likely the true one, he thinks, for the Hebrew for "oxen" (bekarim) may be a corruption of that for "gourds" or "grapes" (pekaim), the letters being so readily mistaken in old manuscripts that "grapes might be turned into oxen." He canvasses Houbigant, who defends both texts by taking the word for a large kind of grape, but finds no warrant for that sense and prefers the simpler supposition of a change of letters. Clarke adds the sensible caution that these ornamental oxen round the brim are wholly different from the twelve oxen of the next verse that bear the sea up.

Charles Ellicott resists the whole assumption. There is no reason, he argues, unless we suppose each writer meant to give an exhaustive description — which neither did — why the ornamental rows should not have included both features, small figures of oxen as well as wild gourds. To Reuss's objection that oxen "ten in a cubit" would be impossibly small, he answers that such miniature work was by no means beyond ancient art, pointing to the reliefs on the bronze doors of Shalmaneser and to the analogous ornament of 1 Kings 7:29; and he suggests it was rather a copyist of the older book who inadvertently repeated the word "gourds" from 1 Kings 6:18. It is "sheer dogmatism," he says, to assert that the chronicler's copyists absurdly changed the gourds into oxen.

Keil and Delitzsch stand with the first party, treating the chronicler's "oxen" as one of the orthographical slips to be restored from the older text, while they carry the description forward to the great oxen that bear the sea. So the debate is real and unresolved: was the little band of oxen a scribe's corruption of the gourds, to be corrected away, or an authentic second ornament that the fuller original simply happened to omit? The witnesses divide along the line that runs through the whole chapter — how far the Chronicler's divergences are a scribe's errors, and how far they are the record's own.
4:
The great basin did not rest on the ground but was lifted upon twelve oxen of bronze — three looking toward the north, three toward the west, three toward the south, and three toward the east, their hinder parts turned inward and the sea set above them. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the witness who weighs the verse most closely, finds in it a small but telling proof of the chronicler's method: the whole verse coincides verbally with the parallel in 1 Kings 7:25, with a single slight exception — for the common form of the numeral "twelve" he has substituted the rare form. It is exactly the kind of trace the grammatical-historical eye looks for: not a difference of substance but of spelling, a later writer copying an older text and lightly modernizing a word as he went, so that even the count of the oxen is preserved unchanged while the shape of the word is not.

Keil and Delitzsch, describing the sea across these verses, set the same twelve oxen before us with their faces to the four quarters and their bodies gathered inward beneath the load. Neither witness presses a hidden meaning upon the number; they are content to fix the arrangement and the reading. Yet the plain description is not without its own weight. The one great laver of the priests is borne up, not by pillars or a bare stand, but by living figures set toward every point of the compass, the whole heavy circle of water resting upon them — the vessel of cleansing established at the centre of the court and facing outward to all the world around it.

The witnesses leave the twelve where the text sets them, three to each wind, and the reader is left to feel the deliberate symmetry of a house whose very furniture was ordered by measure and number. What the grammatical-historical voices establish here is the trustworthiness of that record: down to the bronze bodies of the oxen and the direction of their faces, the Chronicler has handed on faithfully what he received, and the tiny variant in a single word only shows how closely he was copying. The account does not moralize the twelve; it numbers them, sets them to the four winds, and lets the sheer order of the thing testify to the care with which the house of God was furnished.
5:
A handbreadth thick, its brim wrought like the brim of a cup and flowered with lilies, the sea "received and held three thousand baths" — and that number sets the witnesses at odds, for the older account in 1 Kings 7:26 gives its capacity as two thousand. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes the discrepancy for a real one and looks for a real reconciliation. Chronicles was written after the Babylonian captivity, he reasons, so the measure here may be the Babylonish bath, smaller than the ancient Jewish one, just as he had argued the older Hebrew cubit was longer; two thousand of the old baths might equal three thousand of those used after the exile. Failing that, he reports the Targum's neat solution — that the vessel "received three thousand baths of dry measure, and held two thousand of liquid measure."

The grammatical-historical witnesses prefer to lay the difference at a copyist's door. Charles Ellicott notes that the description of the thickness and the cup-like, lily-fringed brim is word for word identical with the older account, so that only the number has moved; and he weighs two ways it might have slipped — a scribe reading "thousands" where the original had the dual "two thousand," then making the vague word definite by adding "three," or else the numeral "three" dropping out of the older text and its indefinite "thousands" being hardened into "two thousand." Either mistake was possible, he observes, because in the unpointed Hebrew the two forms are written alike. Keil and Delitzsch agree, ranking the three thousand among the orthographical errors of the Chronicle, and defending the seemingly superfluous verb "it held" as no gloss but a mere pleonasm.

So the debate stands drawn: is the sea's larger capacity a true figure explained by a later, smaller measure, as Clarke maintains, or simply a number corrupted in transmission, as Ellicott and Keil conclude? Matthew Henry, characteristically, steps past the arithmetic to the meaning. Filled to the brim the vessel would hold three thousand baths, though ordinarily it stood at two thousand; and the Holy Ghost signified by so vast a laver the great gospel privilege that "the blood of Christ cleanseth from all sin," as 1 John 1:7 declares — a fountain opened, wide as the sea, for all who by faith apply to it for the purifying of their consciences. The puritan-evangelical voice thus turns even a textual crux toward the fulness of merit in Christ, while the grammatical-historical witnesses keep watch over the letter and the number.
6:
Ten bronze lavers now join the great sea, five set on the right hand and five on the left, and the verse marks a clear division of labour: in the lavers "such things as they offered for the burnt offering they washed," but the sea itself "was for the priests to wash in." Adam Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian witness, states the arrangement plainly — the ten lavers served to rinse the several parts of the victims, while the molten sea was for the priests' own use, in which they bathed or from which they drew water for their personal purification. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the philology and notes what the chronicler has done: the word for laver is used elsewhere of a cooking-pan and of a pan holding fire, and means here a pan for washing; and he observes that the sacred writer, returning to his abbreviating style, has passed over altogether the ten wheeled bronze stands described in curious detail in 1 Kings 7:27, judging them of less importance than the golden furniture he means to insert next, or else daunted by the difficulty of the passage. Keil and Delitzsch, carrying their description across these verses, likewise identify the ten lavers as those which, in the fuller account of 1 Kings 7:38, rested upon ten brazen chests fitted with carriage-wheels.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the division itself as instruction. As the priests must be washed, he says, so must the sacrifices; and the ten lavers in which the offerings were rinsed teach us not only to purify ourselves in preparation for our religious duties but carefully to put away "all those vain thoughts and corrupt aims which cleave to our performances themselves and pollute them." The great sea he had already taken for the gospel duty that answers its privilege — that those who draw nigh to God must cleanse their hands and purify their hearts, as James 4:8 requires, and that he who is washed still needs to wash his feet, to renew his repentance whenever he goes in to minister, after the pattern of John 13:10.

The witnesses do not divide. The grammatical-historical pair fix what the lavers were, where they stood, and what the Chronicler chose to omit; Clarke sorts out their several uses; and Henry draws from the two kinds of washing — of the priest and of the sacrifice — the double lesson that both the worshipper and his very worship must be cleansed before a holy God.
7:
With the golden furniture the account moves from the court into the holy place, and the ten golden candlesticks are the first of its treasures. Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witness, remark at once that these three verses have no counterpart in the parallel narrative of 1 Kings 7, where the candlesticks and the table of shewbread are named only later in the summary list, and where the outer court goes unmentioned; no reason can be given for the omission, they say, but that the verses dropped out. Charles Ellicott renders the phrase "according to their form" as "according to their prescribed rule," comparing the summary of 1 Kings 7:49 and the pattern of the one lampstand laid down in Exodus 25:31, and fixes their place "before the chancel," at the entrance of the inner sanctuary. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gives the same phrase its plainest sense: "their form" is the old form which God prescribed to Moses — the ten new lamps were shaped after the one ancient pattern.

The multiplication from one lampstand to ten draws the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry into his richest typology. The written word, he says, is a lamp and a light shining in a dark place; in Moses' time they had but one candlestick, the Pentateuch, but the additions that in process of time would be made of the other books of Scripture might be signified by this increase in the number of the lamps — "Light was growing." And the candlesticks, he adds on the warrant of Revelation 1:20, are the churches: Moses set up but one, the church of the Jews, whereas in the gospel temple not only believers but whole churches are multiplied. The nearer we come to God, Henry observes of all this inner gold, the purer we must be, and the purer we shall be.

The witnesses agree without strain. Keil marks the silence of the older book and the fact of the omission; Ellicott and Wesley secure the sense of "their form" as the Mosaic pattern faithfully reproduced; and Henry reads in the growth from one lamp to ten both the swelling canon of Scripture and the spreading light of the churches — the single flame of the old covenant multiplied, without loss of its pattern, into the many lamps of the new.
8:
Ten golden tables answer the ten candlesticks, five on the right and five on the left, and with them a hundred golden basins — and the witnesses busy themselves with the use of both. Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical voice, correct a natural guess: the tables were not made to bear the candlesticks but to hold the shewbread, and the hundred basins were not for catching and sprinkling sacrificial blood but, as their pairing with the shewbread tables shows, wine-flagons or sacrificial vessels for the drink-offerings, a sense they say is put beyond doubt by Amos 6:6. Charles Ellicott allows that the golden lampstands may after all have stood upon these tables, but agrees the basins were bowls for pouring libations, notes that the Syriac and Arabic reckon them a hundred and twenty, and observes the chronicler's characteristic habit: where the older text speaks of one table only, the table of shewbread named in 1 Kings 7:48, he consistently writes of tables in the plural. Adam Clarke takes the basins for a sort of paterae, the shallow sacrificial dishes with which libations were made.

Here too the wesleyan-arminian and puritan-evangelical witnesses turn the furniture toward its meaning, and they meet on the same thought. John Wesley notes that on these tables the shewbread was set — perhaps each of the ten bearing its twelve loaves — and draws the lesson that "as the house was enlarged, so was the provision." Matthew Henry says the same and lifts it higher: as the temple was greater than the tabernacle, so was its housekeeping; the enlarged table is a sign that "in my Father's house there is bread enough for the whole family," and the hundred golden basins that belonged to it show that God's table is well furnished. Henry hears in the multiplied loaves and vessels the abundance of the gospel provision, spread now not for one but for all.

The witnesses do not contend; the small differences — a hundred basins or a hundred and twenty, one table in the older record or ten here, whether the lamps stood on the tables — are matters of number and arrangement, not of substance. What Keil and Ellicott settle by philology, that the basins held wine for the libations and the tables held the bread of the Presence, Wesley and Henry translate into promise: the house of God is a house of plentiful bread, and its enlargement under Solomon is a shadow of the fuller table yet to be spread in Christ.
9:
Beyond the vessels the account turns to the enclosures — the court of the priests, the great court, and the doors that closed it, their leaves overlaid with bronze. Adam Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian witness, distinguishes the two plainly: the court of the priests was the inner court, and the great court the outer, the place where the body of the people assembled. Charles Ellicott, the grammatical-historical voice, dwells on the words. "The great court" translates a late Hebrew term common in the Targums for the older word just used — the inner court of 1 Kings 6:36 — and denotes the outer court, which the parallel narrative does not mention at all; and the plating of the doors with bronze he illustrates from the monuments — the bronze-sheathed doors of Shalmaneser's palace at Balawat stood twenty-two feet high, each leaf six feet wide. Keil and Delitzsch note only that the two courts are not further described, and that the leaves of the outer doors were overlaid with copper; and they carry the reader on to the heavier bronze utensils of the altar — the pots for the ashes, the shovels, and the basins for the sacrificial blood — which the verses that follow will credit to Huram.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads even the doors toward edification. They were overlaid with brass, he says, both for strength and for beauty, and that they might not be rotted by the weather to which they were exposed; and he recalls the "gates of brass" of Psalms 107:16, which God is praised for breaking. The bronze that guarded the courts was a fit emblem of the durable strength of the house of God, well able to bear the exposure of its station.

The witnesses do not divide. Clarke and Ellicott settle which court is which and recover the outer court that the older book had passed over; Keil marks the plain copper of the doors and threads the narrative on to the altar-vessels; and Henry finds in the very plating a lesson of strength and endurance. What the chapter builds outward, from the golden furniture of the inner house to the bronze-clad doors of the outer court, the witnesses read as one ordered whole — the enlarged worship of an enlarged people fenced and adorned to the last leaf of its gates.
10:
A single note of placement fixes where the great sea stood: "on the right side of the east end, over against the south." Charles Ellicott, the grammatical-historical witness, renders the Hebrew literally — "on the right shoulder, eastward, in front of the southward" — and reads it as the south-east side of the house, agreeing with the position given in the older account at 1 Kings 7:39. He notes that the Septuagint and some manuscripts add the words "of the house," which appear to have fallen out of the received text — one more small witness to the wear a text takes in copying. Keil and Delitzsch place the same half-verse as a supplementary statement of the sea's position, coinciding with the parallel, and take it as the hinge on which the account turns from the individual furnishings to a summary catalogue. For with the next words the heavier bronze work is gathered up: Huram made the pots for carrying off the ashes, the shovels for lifting them from the altar, and the basins for catching and sprinkling the blood — and though Huram's name stands over them here, the same utensils reappear in the summary list, so that the notice belongs, they observe, with what precedes.

Brief as it is, the verse rewards the grammatical-historical care the witnesses bring to it. The sea is not merely made but stationed, set deliberately at the south-eastern corner where the priests coming and going would find it ready to their hand; and the placing of so vast a vessel, like the placing of the altar and the tables and the lamps, is part of the ordered exactness with which the whole house was furnished. Even the small quarrel over two lost words — whether the text once read "the right shoulder of the house" — is not idle, for it belongs to the larger question the chapter keeps raising, how faithfully the record has come down and where a copyist's hand has touched it.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott recovers the lost words and fixes the compass-point; Keil marks the verse as the seam between the detailed description and the inventory that follows. Between them they show the Chronicler moving, with a copyist's fidelity and an editor's economy, from the sea and its oxen to the long roll of vessels that Huram wrought and Solomon multiplied for the house of God — the account gathering itself, at this turn, for the summary that the next verses will unfold.
11:
With Huram's name the account pauses to praise the workman and the king. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on two virtues. First the craftsman's fidelity: "he finished all that he was to make," leaving no part of the work undone. Henry lingers over the title "Huram his father," by which the artificer is called at the sixteenth verse — probably a kind of affectionate nickname, "Father Huram," for the king of Tyre had styled him Huram Abi, "my father," and Solomon in courtesy did the same, honouring him as a great master and father of the workers in brass and iron; he acquitted himself well, Henry says, for both ingenuity and industry. Second the king's generosity: Solomon made all the vessels in great abundance, many of a sort, that many hands might be employed and the work go forward, or that some might be laid up against the wearing out of others. "Freely he has received, and he will freely give"; and when enough had been made for present use, Henry notes, Solomon would not turn the remaining brass to his own profit — it was devoted to God, and for God it should be used.

Charles Ellicott, the grammatical-historical witness, works at the text of the same verse. Where the older account reads "lavers," our text has "pots" — scuttles for carrying away the ashes of the altar — and Ellicott judges the chronicler's reading correct, supported by many manuscripts and the ancient versions, the two words differing by a single stroke in Hebrew. The "basons," he adds, are bowls, likely the same vessels named a few verses before; the workman's name is written Hiram in the Hebrew, as in the older book; and the clause the Authorized Version gives as "that he was to make" is better rendered simply "he made," the chronicler supplying a preposition that older usage did not require.

The two witnesses do not divide, and their two labours meet. Ellicott secures what the verse says — that Huram wrought the pots and shovels and bowls and finished all the work he made for Solomon for the house of God — and Henry draws from that finished work its double example: a workman who left nothing undone, and a king whose abundance was all devoted, holding back none of the consecrated metal for himself. Together they make the summary notice, easily passed over, a small lesson in faithful service and open-handed giving to the house of the Lord.
12:
The summary of Huram's bronze-work now runs through the great pair of pillars and their crowns — "the two pillars, and the pommels, and the chapiters which were on the top of the pillars, and the two wreaths to cover the two pommels." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott clears the archaic English. "Pommels" and "chapiters" are the globes and the capitals; the Hebrew word the Authorized Version renders "bowls" in the older account is in fact the same word, meaning globes, so that the phrase "the globes of the capitals" there is plainly wrong. He attends even to the grammar of the number "two," which is feminine and so agrees with the feminine "globes and capitals" rather than with the masculine "pillars" — it is two globes and capitals that are meant. The "wreaths," he adds, are lattices, the word the same as that rendered "network" in 1 Kings 7:41.

Keil and Delitzsch, carrying their catalogue across these verses, gather the whole roll of Huram's brass: the two pillars with their globes and capitals and the two lattice-wreaths that covered them; the four hundred pomegranates in two rows upon the wreaths; the bases and their lavers; the one sea and the twelve oxen beneath it; the pots and shovels and the rest of the instruments, all wrought of bright brass. They mark where the casting was done — in the plain of Jordan, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zeredathah — and how the whole was made in such abundance that the weight of the brass could not be found out. Their interest is in the completeness of the inventory, the ordered sum of everything the Tyrian master had cast for the house of God.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott sharpens the sense of the several words and corrects the older English where it stumbled; Keil holds the long list together and traces it to the foundries by the Jordan. Between them the two pillars — the Jachin and Boaz of the older narrative, though the chronicler here only numbers their parts — take their place in the great catalogue of bronze, their globed capitals wreathed in lattice-work and hung with pomegranates, the most conspicuous of all the vessels that stood before the enlarged house of the Lord. The account, gathering item to item, presses on toward the reckoning of gold that the closing verses will bring.
13:
"And four hundred pomegranates on the two wreaths; two rows of pomegranates on each wreath, to cover the two pommels of the chapiters." The ornament of the pillar-capitals is told in a single crowded verse, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott touches it with the briefest of notes, sending the reader for the parallel to 1 Kings 7:42, where the same four hundred pomegranates in their two rows are numbered. The economy of the comment is itself instructive: where the older account and the chronicler agree so exactly, down to the count of the fruit and the doubling of the rows, there is nothing for the grammatical-historical eye to correct, and the witness is content simply to lay the two texts side by side and let their agreement stand.

Yet the verse repays a moment's dwelling on the thing described. Two lattice-wreaths of bronze were flung over the twin globes that crowned the pillars, and upon each wreath ran two rows of pomegranates, two hundred to a pillar, four hundred in all — a girdle of cast fruit encircling the capitals where they stood at the porch of the house. The pomegranate, with its crowded seeds, was the old adornment of the high priest's robe and a standing emblem of fruitfulness and abundance; here it is multiplied by hundreds and lifted high on the bronze that flanked the temple door, so that the worshipper passing in walked beneath a double crown of fruit. The number is not idle: it is part of the same ordered exactness that set twelve oxen beneath the sea and ten lamps before the sanctuary, the house of God furnished throughout by measure and count.

The single witness does not divide against himself, and there is no other here to contend with him. Ellicott fixes the reading and the reference; the rest is the plain testimony of the verse — that the pillars of the porch were wreathed with four hundred pomegranates, ranked two rows deep upon each capital, the most richly ornamented feature of all the bronze-work. What the older narrative recorded of Solomon's pillars the Chronicler faithfully repeats, adding nothing and losing nothing, so that the fruited capitals stand in his account exactly as they stood before the house they adorned.
14:
"He made also bases, and lavers made he upon the bases." The verse belongs to the summary roll of Huram's work, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds in its very wording a mark of the text's history. The repetition of the verb — "he made... made he" — is suspicious, he says, and the parallel account shows what the right reading must have been: "and the bases ten in number, and the lavers ten upon the bases." The Hebrew word for "ten" closely resembles the word for "he made," so that a copyist's eye, sliding from the one to the other, could easily have turned the count of the bases and lavers into a bare repetition of the verb. The Septuagint, he notes, already reproduces the doubled "he made," which shows that the corruption of the text is ancient — older than the Greek translators themselves.

It is a small textual observation, but it opens the whole grammatical-historical method the chapter keeps rewarding. The bases here are the ten wheeled stands whose elaborate structure the older narrative had described at length in 1 Kings 7:27 and which the chronicler, in his abbreviating way, has passed over, keeping only their bare mention in the inventory; and the lavers are the ten bronze basins that rested upon them, in which the parts of the burnt offerings were rinsed. Where the fuller record gave the number and the workmanship, the summary here gives only that they were made — and even that little, Ellicott shows, has suffered in the copying, the lost "ten" surviving in the parallel but not in our text.

The witness does not divide against himself, and no other stands with him on the verse. Ellicott simply restores what the letter had blurred: not one act of making vaguely repeated, but ten bases and ten lavers set down in their proper count. The correction is characteristic of the whole chapter's interest — how faithfully, and yet through what human accidents of transmission, the record of the temple furniture has come down; and it leaves the bases and their lavers standing, ten and ten, in the great catalogue of bronze that Huram wrought and Solomon multiplied for the house of God, their number recovered from the older text even where the Chronicler's own copy had let it slip.
15:
"One sea, and twelve oxen under it." The great laver and its bearers, already described in their place, are here numbered once more in the summary of Huram's bronze, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the terse form of the reckoning. The Hebrew reads literally "the sea one" and "the oxen twelve under it," where the older account had written "the one sea" and "the twelve oxen under the sea"; the chronicler, Ellicott observes, has abridged the expression, dropping the repeated noun and leaving only the pronoun "it." The compression is slight, but it is exactly the kind of economy the whole chapter shows — the summary catalogue clipping the fuller phrases of the older record for the sake of a swifter inventory.

The verse gains its weight from what it gathers up. The sea that was described at length near the chapter's opening — ten cubits from brim to brim, a handbreadth thick, its lily-fringed brim holding its vast measure of water — is here set down in three words among the rest of the bronze, and the twelve oxen that bore it, three to each quarter of the compass, are numbered again beneath it. The single sea and its twelve supporters are named together, as they stood together in the court, the one great vessel of cleansing lifted upon the ranked figures that faced outward to every wind. In the roll of Huram's work the sea takes its place beside the pillars and the pomegranates and the lavers, one item among many, yet the largest and most striking of them all, so that the eye running down the inventory cannot miss it.

The witness does not divide, and no other joins him here. Ellicott fixes the abridged wording and lets the older text supply what the chronicler has trimmed. What the summary states so briefly the earlier verses had unfolded in full, and the two together — the detailed description and the clipped inventory — show the Chronicler's twofold habit: to dwell on the furniture where it first appears, and then to sweep it up, shortened and numbered, into the closing catalogue of all that was cast in bronze for the house of the Lord.
16:
"The pots also, and the shovels, and the fleshhooks, and all their instruments, did Huram his father make to king Solomon." Two questions occupy the witnesses: what the utensils were, and why the workman is called "his father." On the first, the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott suspects that "fleshhooks" should rather be "bowls," the word differing by little from that so rendered elsewhere, though he allows that Exodus 27:3 lists pots and shovels and bowls and fleshhooks together among the altar's furniture, so that both words may well belong here. He fixes "bright" as "polished," comparing the burnished bronze of Jeremiah 46:4, and denies that "their instruments" could mean the moulds in which the vessels were cast — for moulds would not be made of polished brass.

On the title "his father" the wesleyan-arminian voices speak with one mind. Adam Clarke explains that the Hebrew word ab, father, is often used for a master, an inventor, a chief operator, and is probably so used here, as the Chaldee took it — "all these Chiram his master made for King Solomon." John Wesley says the same more warmly: Huram is called Solomon's father because the king was wont to call him so "out of that great respect which he bare to him for his excellent art and service," it being usual to honour great artists and inventors with that name. The workman is no kinsman of the king, but a master of his craft dignified with a title of reverence.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott weighs the vessels and the reading, recovering the polished bronze and the likely bowls; Clarke and Wesley agree that "father" is a name of honour for a master-craftsman, not a claim of blood. Between them they leave the verse clear: Huram, the Tyrian master whom Solomon honoured as a father in the craft, made the pots and shovels and bowls and all their instruments of bright, polished brass for the house of the Lord — the last of the bronze-work before the account turns to the place of its casting and the reckoning of its weight.
17:
"In the plain of Jordan did the king cast them, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zeredathah." The verse fixes the foundry, and the witnesses attend to its soil and its place-names. Adam Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian voice, glances at the older account of the same casting and reports a difference of opinion: some suppose that Solomon did not actually cast the great vessels on the spot but brought the clay from that quarter, as being the fittest for making the moulds into which the molten brass was run. Charles Ellicott, the grammatical-historical witness, renders "clay ground" literally as "the thickness of the ground" — that is, the stiff or clayey soil — comparing the word's use in Job 15:26, and noting that the older narrative uses a synonym found nowhere else. On the second place-name he shows that "Zeredathah" and the "Zarethan" of Joshua 3:16 denote the same spot, the form here meaning "toward Zeredah," the town named in 1 Kings 11:26.

The point on which the account and its interpreters converge is the fitness of the ground for the work. The Jordan plain between Succoth and Zeredathah offered a dense, clayey earth that would take and hold the shape of a mould without crumbling or letting the metal drain away — the very quality Clarke's authorities prize, whether the casting was done there or only the clay dug there. It was in this humble river-bottom, far from the temple mount, that the pillars and the sea and the oxen and all the great bronzes were founded, then carried up to Jerusalem to take their places in the house of God.

The witnesses do not divide. Clarke reports the question whether the vessels were cast or only their moulds prepared in the plain; Ellicott settles the sense of the soil and the identity of the two towns. Between them they anchor the shining furniture of the temple to a particular stretch of clay by the Jordan, and remind the reader that the glory which would stand in the sanctuary was first shaped, unseen, in the stiff earth of a distant valley — the grammatical-historical care for a place-name opening, almost in passing, upon the long labour that lay behind the finished house.
18:
"Thus Solomon made all these vessels in great abundance: for the weight of the brass could not be found out." The verse rounds off the roll of bronze with a note of sheer profusion, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott compares it closely with the older account, which reads rather that "Solomon left all the vessels unweighed, from very great abundance." The difference, he suggests, may be due to a copyist whose eye wandered to the opening of the next verse; but it is also possible that the chronicler simply missed the force of the verb the older record used — "left unweighed" — and therefore put in its place an easier term, "made." The further small changes, he adds — "unto great abundance," "for the weight," and the like — point the same way, toward a writer smoothing a phrase he found difficult. The closing clause he renders exactly: the weight of the brass "was not ascertained."

Whichever account of the wording is right, the sense the witness leaves standing is the same, and it is a striking one. So vast was the quantity of bronze that Huram cast and Solomon devoted that no reckoning of its weight was ever taken; the metal was beyond counting, left unweighed for its very abundance. The detail belongs with the earlier note that the vessels were made "many of a sort," and with the whole tenor of the chapter, which sets an enlarged people bringing an enlarged worship to an enlarged house — the offerings so many that the great altar was widened to hold them, the bronze so heaped that the scales were never brought.

The witness does not divide against himself, and no other stands with him here. Ellicott weighs only the letter — whether Solomon "made" or "left unweighed," and how the chronicler's phrase came to differ from the older — and lets the plain magnitude of the thing speak for itself. What the summary of Huram's brass had numbered item by item it here sums up in a single confession of excess: the vessels of the Lord's house were past weighing, and the abundance that furnished them was past finding out. On that note of unmeasured plenty the account of the bronze closes, and the last verses turn to the purer treasure of gold.
19:
With the gold the account enters the holy place proper — "all the vessels that were for the house of God, the golden altar also, and the tables whereon the shewbread was set." Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witness, note that here the text diverges a little further from the older summary, especially over the tables of shewbread, and that the number and position of the candlesticks are not repeated, having been given already; instead their use is stressed — to be lighted, as the ordinance required, before the most holy place. Charles Ellicott presses the recurring difficulty of the tables: the chronicler consistently speaks of tables in the plural, where the older record names but one table of shewbread, and yet elsewhere the same writer can speak of a single "pure table"; the likeliest account, Ellicott thinks, is that, finding several tables mentioned in one of his sources, the chronicler grouped them all with the table of the Presence, gaining brevity at some cost of exactness. He recalls too the eight tables of hewn stone in Ezekiel 40:39, whereon the victims were slain.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the golden altar toward its antitype. This was the altar of incense, he says, probably enlarged in proportion to the brazen altar of sacrifice; and he reads it of Christ, who, having once for all made atonement for sin, "ever lives, making intercession, in virtue of that atonement." As the bronze altar in the court had spoken of the propitiation, so the golden altar within speaks of the intercession that flows from it — the two altars together shadowing the one Mediator's finished sacrifice and unceasing prayer.

The witnesses do not divide. Keil and Ellicott labour over the letter — the stressed use of the lamps, the puzzle of the one table or the many — and trace the chronicler's compressions and groupings back to his sources; Henry reads the golden altar as the standing sign of Christ's intercession. Between the grammatical-historical care for the text and the puritan care for its meaning, the verse yields both its history and its gospel: the enlarged golden furniture of the sanctuary, faithfully if briefly catalogued, and at its heart the altar of incense on which the whole later doctrine of the great High Priest's intercession would rest.
20:
The lampstands return, now with a word about their burning: "the candlesticks with their lamps, that they should burn after the manner before the oracle, of pure gold." Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witness, take the lamps to be lit, as the ordinance required, before the inner sanctuary, and reckon with the small implements named alongside — the knives or snuffers used to trim the wicks, which are not found among the utensils of the tabernacle but appear here and in the later temple inventories of 2 Kings 12:14 and Jeremiah 52:18. Charles Ellicott observes that the clause "that they should burn after the manner" — according to the legal rule — is the chronicler's own addition, and that in return he omits the older account's detail of "five on the right hand and five on the left," having already given the number and the placing at the seventh verse. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley glosses "the manner" in his brief way: it is "according to the prescription of God to Moses," the lamps burning by the old rule laid down for the tabernacle.

The witnesses agree, and the point they make together is one of continuity. The ten golden lampstands of Solomon's enlarged house were no innovation of worship but the ancient Mosaic lamp multiplied and set to burn by the ancient Mosaic rule; what had shone before the veil in the wilderness shines now, ten times over, before the oracle of the temple. Even the little tools of the service — the snuffers and knives of pure gold — are noted, small signs of the exactness with which the light was tended.

Keil fixes the implements and their later history; Ellicott marks what the chronicler adds and what he leaves out, and refers the number back to the earlier verse; Wesley secures the sense of "the manner" as God's own prescription. There is no dispute among them. The verse, brief as it is, gathers the golden lamps of the sanctuary into the same pattern the whole chapter has drawn — the worship of an enlarged people carried on by an enlarged furniture, yet still ordered, lamp and snuffer and all, "after the manner" God had appointed from the first. The light grows in quantity while its rule stays unchanged.
21:
The finishing touches of the golden furniture are told in a crowd of small vessels — "the flowers, and the lamps, and the tongs, made he of gold, and that perfect gold." Adam Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian witness, pictures the workmanship: probably each branch of the golden lampstand was wrought like a plant in flower, and the opening of the flower either was the lamp itself or served to support it, so that light seemed to blossom from a stem of gold. Charles Ellicott, the grammatical-historical voice, weighs the phrase "that perfect gold," rendering it "it was perfection of gold," and notes that the rare intensive word occurs nowhere else in Scripture; the little touch, added to heighten the effect, is quite in the chronicler's manner and is not to be suspected as a late intrusion. He identifies the several implements that follow — the snuffers are shears or scissors for trimming the wicks, and the "spoons and censers" are rather trays and snuff-dishes — and turns at last to the doors, "the entry of the house," which he explains as including both the doors of the nave or holy place and those of the inner shrine.

Over one word here the grammatical-historical eye lingers. The older account speaks of the "hinges" of the doors, using a term found nowhere else in the Bible, and some have thought our "entry" a corruption of it; but Ellicott reasons the other way. It is likely enough that the temple doors were plated with gold, he says, but hardly that their very hinges were made of gold, and the unique word for "hinges" may not even be genuine — so that the chronicler's "entry" need not be corrected away.

The witnesses do not divide. Clarke dwells on the flowering beauty of the lamps; Ellicott secures the sense of "perfect gold," names the trays and snuffers and snuff-dishes, and defends the reading of the doors. Between them they bring the golden inventory to its close — every flower and lamp and pair of tongs, every tray and dish and door, wrought of the purest gold, "perfection of gold," for the innermost house of the Lord. The nearer the account draws to the holy of holies, the purer the metal it names, until nothing is left but gold and the doors that open upon the most holy place.
22:
The last verse gathers the remaining gold — "the snuffers, and the basons, and the spoons, and the censers, of pure gold" — and closes with the doors: "the entry of the house, the inner doors thereof for the most holy place, and the doors of the house of the temple, were of gold." Adam Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian witness who weighs the verse, explains that the doors were of gold in the sense of being overlaid with golden plates, though of what thickness we cannot know. From that plating he passes to a larger reflection on how the whole temple is to be read. That everything in the tabernacle and temple was typical, representative of some excellence of the gospel dispensation, may be readily believed, he says, without following the pious author of "Solomon's Temple Spiritualized" into all his detail. We may see the general reference and the principles of the great design, and yet not be able to fasten a particular gospel doctrine upon the knops, the flowers, the pomegranates, the tongs, and the snuffers.

Here Clarke sets a real caution against the very kind of reading that other witnesses have pressed elsewhere in the chapter. Such minute spiritualizing, he warns, is "in most cases weak, silly, religious trifling, being ill calculated to produce respect for Divine revelation." The furniture may be typical in its general design without every hook and blossom carrying a hidden meaning; to hunt a doctrine in each utensil is to cheapen the Scripture one means to honour.

The single witness does not divide against himself, and none stands to contend with him on the verse, yet his note quietly answers the more exuberant typology the chapter has drawn from its brazen altar, its molten sea, and its golden lamps. Clarke does not deny that the house of God shadowed forth the things of the gospel; he grants the general reference and the principles of the design. He only refuses to press the shadow into every corner. So the account of the temple furniture ends on a golden threshold — the very doors of the most holy place sheathed in pure gold — and with a sober word of proportion: the reader may lift his eyes from the gold to the glory it foreshadowed, without turning every flower and snuffer into a sermon. The house is a type; but it is the great design, not the smallest detail, that the wise interpreter reads.
